markdownRemark.excerpt: What we will make? We’re gonna create a Game Management List! You can store all your favorite games in one list for easy reference, you can…
markdownRemark.html:
What we will make?

We’re gonna create a Game Management List! You can store all your favorite games in one list for easy reference, you can review them yourself, give it scores, comments and the console you used! It will consist of a table connected to a toolbar that will have a searchbar to filter the games and a button which brings a modal with a form inside that will let us add games to the table. To delete it we will add on each row a checkbox input that will change the button to bring a delete form instead.

You might be wondering, don’t we need to create a backend server for all this? For now we will focus with Frontend development and our job is to make use of the information provided by the Backend, so instead of a traditional server, we will use a tool called JSON Server, which will let us simulate a backend server in a very simple way!

This is important because we want to be using Redux Thunk on this project for making async calls.

Lets get started!

Creating our enviroment.

We will use Create React App for this project:

npx create-react-app react-redux-games-list-management
cd react-redux-games-list-management
npm start

We erase all the files inside src folder until it looks like this:

  • src

    • App.js
    • index.js
    • serviceWorker.js

Now lets install redux and axios for async!

yarn add redux react-redux axios

Now lets build our folder’s structure, since its a small project, we will keep it simple for now.

  • src

    • actions

    • components

      • button
      • modal
      • searchbar
      • table
      • toolbar
    • pages
    • reducers
    • store
    • types
    • App.js
    • index.js

For store we will rewrite it like this:

From Redux we get createStore which is self explanatory, it takes the rootReducer, the initial state, compose which is let us combine functions and applyMiddleware which we use in order to apply Thunk in our app.

Inside index.js we will put the following code:

Provider let us have the global state available for the whole app in this case. We could’ve put all the logic in the folder store inside index.js, but its cleaner to have everything in its rightful place, otherwise it can get messy.

For now this is enough setup to get started with our project in the next section.

Lets create our service!

We’re going to use a tool called JSON Server which will let us create our service which we will use for CRUD (create, read, update, delete) purposes. We will start by installing it:

npm install -g json-server

Now create a file called db.json and put the following data for now:

Start the server with json-server —watch db.json.

Now if we go to http://localhost:3000/games we will see our service right there! Notice that if we change the route to http://localhost:3000/games/1 we will get the first object.

Its a bit painful to write that command every time, so we go to package.json and go inside scripts and add the following:

“server”: “json-server —watch db.json”

This way we can do yarn server and we start the server.

Starting the project!

We be building a game list management system which consists of a simple table with a toolbar on top of it to add, delete or edit games with a searchbar to filter our games by name.

Our first job is to wire the global state to our app, in order to do this we will create first our Action Creators.

Action Creators

We created another folder called types where we have all of our types constants for easy reference and organization. The Action Creators shown are like we saw in our previous post but there is one that’s different isn’t it? That’s basically Redux Thunk in action.

Redux Thunk is a middleware that lets you call action creators that return a function instead of an action object. That function receives the store’s dispatch method, which is then used to dispatch regular synchronous actions inside the body of the function once the asynchronous operations have completed.

So in this case we have a function returning another function with dispatch as argument which we use to tell the reducer we’re gonna start fetching information from the server, we use type: FETCHGAMESLOADING to let it know. Then we use axios and GET to fetch the data, if it results we tell it we were succesful type: FETCHGAMESSUCCESS and send the data to the reducer to do something with it. If it doesn’t result we send type: FETCHGAMESERROR and we send the data for the reducer to handle the error.

Easy right? That’s what Thunk is in a nutshell.

Reducer

Now lets check how our reducer will respond when this action is fired:

You might notice the interesting shape our initial state has, when we send an Action to let it know we’re loading, the reducer reads it and changes the global state to let us know we’re fetching information and we can use a loader in the UI to let the user know data is being fetched, once it results, we put loading as false and we put inside data our information from the service, if it doesn’t fetch we send an error to the console or we could make it show in the UI since this information is available in the whole app.

Structure our page

Lets work on our page before working on our component:

Lets analyze what’s going on:

  • With mapDispatchToProps our page will have the Thunk Action Creator available to fetch information.
  • With mapStateToProps our page will have the global state available.
  • We have a text object we will use as reference for all the texts in our file. Plenty of put texts inside the element directly like classic HTML, but when our component gets bigger and we have to change a text its harder to search for the text in a 2000 line code. So we generally put it at the beginning and we can access it through Javascript, making it also easy to change.
  • ({ fetchGames, loading, data }) here we’re applying some destructuring and we can use props without having to do props.fetchGames.
  • We use lodash get which is one of the most helpful functions since it let us handle falsy values in a more elegant way. The first argument is the object, the second is the path, and the third is an optional default value.
  • useEffect(() => fetchGames(), [fetchGames]), useEffect let us mimic the componentDidMount in this case so we can fetch our information. It takes a second argument which can be an empty array or the dependency, in this case the prop fetchGames. This second argument tells the useEffect when to trigger, if it changes in any form it will cause the effect again. If you don’t pass a second argument you run into an infinite loop.
  • loading, is being given to us by the Redux state in the store, so when we fetch the state changes to loading: true and this element is activated, once the fetch is complete we show the information or handle the error.

If we didn’t use Redux we would had to declare loading and error as local states in the component and having the async function inside the useEffect, making it messy… Imagine we would’ve to do this for many components? Its one of the main factors of Redux being chosen for React projects, its elegant, predictable and easy to make changes to.

Lets work on our Table Component!

Table Component

All components should remain reusable, if you want for it to behave differently then you add more props and provide the change you want. Imagine if we put this component inside our page? What if we needed it somewhere else? Keep things simple and elegant.

The table has a header and a body. We divide it in two different elements recieving their respective prop, in the case of the header we recieve an array of the titles and we map them. On the body we recieve the data we fetched from before which is an array of objects. This is my personal ways of handling components and avoid copy pasting everywhere, while taking full advantage of Javascript.

If we check our local enviroment we will see our table working! But its kinda ugly, for now its ok, so lets wrap up.

Conclusion

This was the first part of this project, we used Redux to handle a simple fetch where our reducer handlers three cases, when we load, when its succesful and when it gives an error. In this case we saw its nothing hard, here are the steps if we want to see it more systematic:

  1. Create a store and surround with a Provider tag to make the global state available.
  2. Create the types as constants in another file.
  3. Create the Action Creators.
  4. Create the Reducer and the different cases it will handle.
  5. Use mapDispathToProps to make the AC available for the page and useEffect to perform a fetch.
  6. Use mapStateToProps to make use of the global state in our page and furthermore in our component.

We created the server with JSON-SERVER which made us create service in less than 30 seconds, in the next post we will add a toolbar where we can add, remove or edit the information of the games using a form inside a modal.

See you on the next post.

Sincerely,

Eng Adrian Beria.

frontmatter.title: Third Project - Redux - Part 2 Toolbar and CRUD
